solve_for: >

  from. Without a clear distinction, governance defaults either to
  too-tight (kills exploratory work) or too-loose (canonical drift).
  This concept locks a SINGLE-AXIS classification — surface only —
  so every artifact has a known governance posture: build-surface
  artifacts get WORKBENCH discipline; operating-surface artifacts get
  full governance. The same vocabulary applies at the thing-level
  (lifecycle_phase: building | operating | archived) reinforcing the
  artifact-level distinction.

# Build-Surface vs Operating-Surface

## The distinction (single axis — SURFACE)

Every artifact in JOS (and every venture) lives on exactly ONE of two
surfaces:

| Surface | What lives here | Governance |
|---|---|---|
| **Build surface** | `WORKBENCH/`, `inbox/`, drafts, research, scratch, riff notes, working files | WORKBENCH discipline (frontmatter + folder rules per [JOS-S32]); freedom to be messy; transient by design |
| **Operating surface** | Canonical body — `decisions/`, `principles/`, `concepts/`, `standards/`, `methods/`, `gates/`, `monitors/`, `specs/`, charter, manifests, README/PURPOSE/VISION/etc. | Full governance — frontmatter REQ, ratification REQ, D-0066 matrix applies, gates fire, persistent by design |

**Binary classification.** A file lives on one surface, not both. The
classification is determined by file location (folder placement).

## Why a single axis (and not two)

An earlier framing proposed TWO axes — surface AND "consultation
phase" (when an artifact is read/used). On inspection this collapsed:
almost every operating-surface artifact is consulted in BOTH phases —
you consult principles while building AND while operating, same for
concepts/standards/decisions/methods/specs/the matrix itself. Saying
"consulted in both phases" became tautological.

**Surface is the only axis that governs anything.** It determines:

- Whether full governance applies (operating: yes; build: WORKBENCH
  discipline only)
- Whether the artifact is promotable (build: yes; operating: already
  promoted)
- Whether it's allowed to be transient (build: yes; operating: no)
- Whether agents/strangers can land cold and find it (operating: yes;
  build: needs context)

Consultation describes how artifacts get used. It's metadata about
behaviour, not a rule. Dropped.

## Directional flow: build → operating

Build-surface work produces operating-surface artifacts through
**promotion events**:

- Strategy work (build) → bets committed to DECISION-LOG +
  sequencing committed to ROADMAP (both operating-surface)
- Research (build) → insights update VISION or trigger new
  DECISIONS (operating)
- Drafts (build) → finished docs promoted to canonical doc-type
  files (operating)
- Discovery (build) → PRINCIPLE/CONCEPT/STANDARD candidates →
  ratified into JOS canonical library via outbox flow (operating)

Governance fires **at promotion events** and **on the operating
surface**. NOT on build-surface content. That's why WORKBENCH stays
zero-ceremony — governance hasn't kicked in yet.

A promotion event is NOT just copy-paste from WORKBENCH to canonical
location. It's a SYNTHESIS: the messy WORKBENCH thinking gets
distilled into a canonical artifact with frontmatter, ratification,
governance.

## The thing-level parallel: `lifecycle_phase`

The same build/operating vocabulary applies at the **thing-level**,
intentionally reinforcing the artifact-level distinction:

| Level | Field | Values | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Artifact level | (implicit, by location) | build-surface / operating-surface | Where this file lives |
| Thing level | `lifecycle_phase:` (in `_manifest.yaml` + charter, per SPEC-009/001) | `building` / `operating` / `archived` | Where this venture/system/product is in its lifecycle |

**Connection:** a thing graduates from `building` to `operating` when
its **operating-SURFACE becomes self-sufficient** — a stranger (human
or agent) can land on this thing and operate it without consulting
build-surface context.

**After graduation, build-surface activity continues forever**
(continuous improvement); operating-surface grows continuously (new
decisions, roadmap updates). Both surfaces are always active in a
healthy thing. Phase just declares "operating surface is now
load-bearing."

The 5 graduation indicators (per [JOS-D0069]) operationalize this
definition: charter ratified, REQ doc-types populated, ≥3 founding
decisions, no WORKBENCH-only operational answers, first-real-use
event recorded.

## Foundry calibration test ✓

Walking the indicators on Foundry-Agent-System: charter ratified ✓,
REQ doc-types populated ✓, D-001 through D-160+ in DECISION-LOG ✓,
operating without build-surface context ✓, in daily production use ✓.

**Foundry is firmly `operating` despite hot build.** Construction
continues; the operating surface is self-sufficient. Correct answer;
this concept's framing holds.

## Implications across JOS

**Implication 1 — D-0066 matrix scope is operating-surface only.**
The doc-type matrix enumerates operating-surface doc-types. Build-
surface artifacts (WORKBENCH, scratch, drafts) are NOT in this matrix
— they're governed by [JOS-S32] WORKBENCH-doc-standard. (See D-0066
preamble update per JOSW2 Group E Item 30.)

**Implication 2 — Strategy is a build-time ACTIVITY, not an operating
doc.** No standalone STRATEGY.md. Strategy work happens in
`WORKBENCH/<person>/proj-strategy-*/`. Outputs flow to DECISION-LOG
(bets), ROADMAP (sequencing), VISION updates (mission reframes). (See
[JOS-D0068].)

**Implication 3 — WORKBENCH stays zero-ceremony.** Light governance
(frontmatter + folder discipline per S32). No matrix, no ratification,
no canonicalization burden. The build surface needs to be a low-
friction space or it doesn't get used.

**Implication 4 — Graduation is identity-changing.** Per [JOS-D0069]
+ JOS-METHOD-GRADUATE-A-THING (formal ratification, Option B locked):
graduation requires a recorded DECISION declaring the transition with
evidence. The `lifecycle_phase` field never auto-flips — human
signoff via decision-log entry is required.

## Connected Objects

- **[JOS-D0067]** — Rule vs Standard Distinction (substrate-taxonomy
  applies on operating surface)
- **[JOS-C46]** — Substrate Taxonomy (all 9 substrate-types live on
  operating surface)
- **[JOS-D0066]** — Doc-Type × Thing-Type Matrix (operating-surface
  scope explicitly declared)
- **[JOS-D0068]** — Strategy as Build-Time Activity
- **[JOS-D0069]** — Thing Graduation Indicators
- **[JOS-S32]** — WORKBENCH-Doc-Standard (build-surface governance)
- **[JOS-METHOD-WORKBENCH-LIFECYCLE]** — promotion events formalized
- **[JOS-METHOD-GRADUATE-A-THING]** — build→operating transition
- **[JOS-SPEC-009]** — Manifest spec adds `lifecycle_phase` field

## Drift-prevention

- **Don't re-introduce a "consultation" axis.** It's degenerate:
  operating-surface is consulted always, build-surface is consulted
  during build. Nothing actually governs differently based on
  consultation. The lone axis is surface.
- **Don't conflate artifact-level surface with thing-level
  lifecycle_phase.** They're parallel concepts using the same
  vocabulary intentionally, but they answer different questions.
  Artifact-level: where does this FILE live? Thing-level: where is
  this WHOLE THING in its lifecycle?
- **Don't apply operating-surface governance to build-surface
  content.** That defeats the zero-ceremony pact and chokes
  exploratory work.
- **Don't try to persist build-surface artifacts as-is.** They're
  meant to be promoted to canonical form (which gets full
  governance) or archived. WORKBENCH content is not the canonical
  surface even if it's been there for years.
- **Don't auto-flip `lifecycle_phase`.** Graduation requires recorded
  DECISION (Option B per D-0069). Future readers should be able to
  grep "when did Foundry graduate?" and find evidence.
- **Don't put strategy in a standalone STRATEGY.md.** Strategy is
  build-time activity. Outputs flow to DECISION-LOG + ROADMAP +
  VISION. (Per [JOS-D0068].)

## Academic / Industry Backing

The build/operating distinction has analogs in established disciplines:

- **Staging vs Production** (software deployment) — same shape:
  staging is messy/transient, production is canonical/governed.
- **Working memory vs Long-term memory** (cognitive science) — work
  happens in a low-friction scratch space; ratified knowledge gets
  encoded into stable storage via a promotion event.
- **Engineering Spike vs Decision Log** (Lean / XP practice) — spikes
  are timeboxed exploration; outputs feed canonical records.
- **Zettelkasten three-stage** (Sönke Ahrens) — fleeting notes →
  literature notes → permanent notes. Promotion events between stages.
- **Notebook vs Library** (publishing) — drafts live in the
  notebook; canonical works live in the library.

The recurring observation: **systems that don't separate the messy-
build space from the canonical-operating space either choke
exploratory work with premature governance, or let canonical drift
in from un-ratified work.** Separating the two surfaces solves both.
